revision.text:
[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A ''katōmado'' ({{nihongo|火灯窓・華頭窓・花頭窓|lit. fire light window}}), also called {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}<ref group="note">This name comes from the presence of such a window in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-[[Ken (architecture)|ma]]|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]].</ref>) is a style of pointed arch window found in Japanese architecture. It originally appeared during the [[Muromachi period]] (1550-58) as an element of [[Zen]] architecture imported from [[China]], but by the end of the 16th century it started to be used by other sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], in [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences.<ref name="jaanus"/> The oldest extant example can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s Shariden in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], and is thought to be identical in style to the Chinese original.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref>
==Notes== <references group="note"/> ==References== <references/>
[[ja:火灯窓]] {{commonscat|Katōmado}}
[[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist art and culture]] [[Category:Zen]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A ''katōmado'' ({{nihongo|火灯窓・華頭窓・花頭窓|lit. fire light window}}), also called {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}<ref group="note">This name comes from the presence of such a window in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-[[Ken (architecture)|ma]]|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]].</ref>) is a style of pointed arch window found in Japanese architecture. It originally appeared during the [[Muromachi period]] (1550-58) as an element of [[Zen]] architecture imported from [[China]], but by the end of the 16th century it started to be used by other sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], in [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences.<ref name="jaanus"/> The oldest extant example can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s Shariden in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], and is thought to be identical in style to the Chinese original.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref>
==Notes== <references group="note"/> ==References== <references/>

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A ''katōmado'' ({{nihongo|火灯窓・華頭窓・花頭窓|lit. fire light window}}), also called {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}<ref group="note">This name comes from the presence of such a window in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-[[Ken (architecture)|ma]]|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]].</ref>) is a style of pointed arch window found in Japanese architecture. It originally appeared during the [[Muromachi period]] (1550-58) as an element of [[Zen]] architecture imported from [[China]], but by the end of the 16th century it started to be used by other sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], in [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences.<ref name="jaanus"/> The oldest extant example can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s Shariden in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], and is thought to be identical in style to the Chinese original.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref>

{{commonscat|Katōmado}} {{Shinto architecture}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist art and culture]] [[Category:Zen]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓・華頭窓・花頭窓|lit. fire light window}}, also called {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}<ref group="note">This name comes from the presence of such a window in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-[[Ken (architecture)|ma]]|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]].</ref> is a style of pointed arch window found in Japanese architecture. It originally appeared during the [[Muromachi period]] in about 1550-58 as an element of [[Zen]] architecture imported from [[China]], but by the end of the 16th century it had started to be used by other sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], in [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus"/> The oldest extant example can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], and is thought to be identical in style to the Chinese original.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{commonscat|Katōmado}} {{Shinto architecture}}
[[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist art and culture]] [[Category:Zen]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓・華頭窓・花頭窓|lit. fire light window}}, also called {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}<ref group="note">This name comes from the presence of such a window in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-[[Ken (architecture)|ma]]|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]].</ref> is a style of pointed arch window found in Japanese architecture. It originally appeared during the [[Muromachi period]] in about 1550-58 as an element of [[Zen]] architecture imported from [[China]], but by the end of the 16th century it had started to be used by other sects of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]], in [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus"/> The oldest extant example can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], and is thought to be identical in style to the Chinese original.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref>
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>
[[ja:火灯窓]] [[ko:화두창]]
{{commonscat|Katōmado}} {{Shinto architecture}} {{Japanese Buddhist architecture}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist art and culture]] [[Category:Zen]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|(with same pronunciation)|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this style of window is also simply called "cusped window", without referring to the particualr Japanese term [[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search]].</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] sects, [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window has changed over time so that the distance between the two vertical frames on the sides became wider and were added with curves at the broad bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for the term have also changed over the long time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a direct cause of hazard against wooden buildings, to a softer combination using "flower" (花).<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563, while the original is believed to have been built sometime between late 14th century and mid 15th century {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another popular example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma]]|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyamadera celebrated its reconstruction, which was realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>
[[ja:火灯窓]] [[ko:화두창]]
{{commonscat|Katōmado}} {{Shinto architecture}} {{Japanese Buddhist architecture}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist art and culture]] [[Category:Zen]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|(with same pronunciation)|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this style of window is also simply called "cusped window", without referring to the particualr Japanese term [[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search]].</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] sects, [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window has changed over time so that the distance between the two vertical frames on the sides became wider and were added with curves at the broad bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for the term have also changed over the long time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a direct cause of hazard against wooden buildings, to a softer combination using "flower" (花).<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Enkaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563,{{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=(Japanese) |format= |work= |accessdate=}} while the original is believed to have been built sometime between late 14th century and mid 15th century {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another popular example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyamadera celebrated its reconstruction, which was realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}
==Notes== <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>
[[ja:火灯窓]] [[ko:화두창]]
{{commonscat|Katōmado}} {{Shinto architecture}} {{Japanese Buddhist architecture}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhist art and culture]] [[Category:Zen]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]]

[[File:Katoumado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' at [[Kōmyō-ji]]]]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|(with same pronunciation)|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this style of window is also simply called "cusped window", without referring to the particualr Japanese term [[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search]].</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] sects, [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window has changed over time so that the distance between the two vertical frames on the sides became wider and were added with curves at the broad bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for the term have also changed over the long time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a direct cause of hazard against wooden buildings, to a softer combination using "flower" (花).<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563,{{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=(Japanese) |format= |work= |accessdate=}} while the original is believed to have been built sometime between late 14th century and mid 15th century {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another popular example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyamadera celebrated its reconstruction, which was realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}

[[File:KamakuraTemple2.jpg|thumb|Engaku-ji Relic Hall with old fashioned ''katōmado's'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|(with same pronunciation)|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this style of window is also simply called "cusped window", without referring to the particualr Japanese term [[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search]].</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] sects, [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window has changed over time so that the distance between the two vertical frames on the sides became wider and were added with curves at the broad bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for the term have also changed over the long time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a direct cause of hazard against wooden buildings, to a softer combination using "flower" (花).<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563,{{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=(Japanese) |format= |work= |accessdate=}} while the original is believed to have been built sometime between late 14th century and mid 15th century {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another popular example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyamadera celebrated its reconstruction, which was realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}
==Notes== [[File:Nanzenji-Katomado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves on side vertical frames]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

called "cusped window", without referring to the particular Japanese term [[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search]].</ref> It first arrived in York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] sects, [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window has changed over time so that the distance from the original meaning "fire" (火), a direct cause of hazard against wooden buildings, to a softer combination using "flower" (花).<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563,{{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=(Japanese) |format= |work= |accessdate=}}, while the original is believed to have been built sometime between late 14th century and mid 15th century {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another popular example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyamadera celebrated its reconstruction, which was realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}
==Notes== [[File:Nanzenji-Katomado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves on side vertical frames]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

revision.text:
[[File:KamakuraTemple2.jpg|thumb|250 px|Engaku-ji,a building with old fashioned ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|(with same pronunciation)|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this style of window is also simply called "cusped window", without referring to the particular Japanese term [[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search]].</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]] sects, [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]], castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window has changed over time so that the distance between the two vertical frames on the sides became wider and were added with curves at the broad bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for the term have also changed over time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a direct cause of hazard against wooden buildings, to a softer combination using "flower" (花).<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563,{{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=(Japanese) |format= |work= |accessdate=}}, while the original is believed to have been built sometime between late 14th century and mid 15th century {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=}}</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another popular example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyamadera celebrated its reconstruction, which was realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}
==Notes== [[File:Nanzenji-Katomado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves on side vertical frames]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>
[[ja:火灯窓]] [[ko:화두창]]

[[File:KamakuraTemple2.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old fashioned ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|with the same pronunciation|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this typr of window is also simply called "cusped window"[http://www.google.co.jp/search?hl=ja&rlz=1T4GPMD_jaJP308JP309&q=zen%20cusped%20window&um=1&ie=UTF-8&tbo=u&tbs=bks:1&source=og&sa=N&tab=wp An example Google Books search].</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, castles, and [[Bushi|samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed over time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a continuos danger for wooden buildings, to a softer combination using the word {{nihongo|"flower"|花}.<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563,{{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}. The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century (source:{{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another renown example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]],<ref group="note">Ishiyama-dera celebrated the reconstruction, realized with the help of [[Yodo-dono]], of its temple including this Main Hall in 1602.</ref> which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}
==Notes== [[File:Nanzenji-Katomado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves on side vertical frames]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

[[File:KamakuraTemple2.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old fashioned ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|with the same pronunciation|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "cusped window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, castles, and [[Bushi|samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> The design and shape of the window changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed over time, from the original meaning "fire" (火), a continuos danger for wooden buildings, to a softer combination using the word {{nihongo|"flower"|花}}.<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of a ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the side vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another renown example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]], which made ''katōmado'' also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}.
==Notes== [[File:Nanzenji-Katomado.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

[[File:KamakuraTemple2.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old fashioned ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "cusped window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of Zen style architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> the window initially wasn't flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}.

[[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "cusped window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, castles, and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> the window initially wasn't flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=6-21-10}}</ref> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}.
==Notes== [[File:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyamadera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}.

The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji]] window}}.
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

[[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=2010-06-21}}</ref> the window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

[[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']]A {{nihongo|'''katōmado'''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in Japanese architecture.<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |pages= |isbn=0-415-10601-X |oclc= |doi= |accessdate=}}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|accessdate=2010-06-21}}</ref> the window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=102&item_id=603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|format= |work= |accessdate=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

{{DEFAULTSORT:Katomado}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Windows]] [[en:Katōmado]][[en:Katōmado]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Katōmado]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Katōmado]][[en:Katōmado]]

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
