revision.text:
{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called "[[wikt:cusped|cusped]] window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called " window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Katōmado}} [[fi:Katōmado]] [[fr:Katōmado]] [[ja:火灯窓]] [[ko:화두창]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called " window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[:en:China|China]] together with [[Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[Zen'yō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Japanese castle|castles]], and [[samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1037421]] Wikidata name: katōmado [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called " window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[:en:China|China]] together with [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[:en:Zenshūyō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, [[:en:Japanese castle|castles]], and [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[:en:Shiga prefecture|Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Katōmado]][[en:Katōmado]][[fi:Katōmado]][[fr:Katōmado]][[ja:火灯窓]][[ko:화두창]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1037421]] Wikidata name: katōmado [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1037421]] Wikidata name: katōmado [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called " window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[:en:China|China]] together with [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[:en:Zenshūyō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, [[:en:Japanese castle|castles]], and [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[:en:Shiga prefecture|Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>
==References== <references/>

Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, [[:en:Japanese castle|castles]], and [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[:en:Shiga prefecture|Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called " window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[:en:China|China]] together with [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[:en:Zenshūyō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, [[:en:Japanese castle|castles]], and [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[:en:Shiga prefecture|Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== [[Image:Shakuzoji Kuginuki Jizo-3601.jpg|thumb|A ''katōmado'' with curves added on the sides]] <references group="note"/>

The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[:en:Shiga prefecture|Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.

wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Katōmado]][[en:Katōmado]][[fi:Katōmado]][[fr:Katōmado]][[ja:火灯窓]][[ko:화두창]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1037421]] Wikidata name: katōmado [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1037421]] Wikidata name: katōmado [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Style of window in Japanese architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Engakuji-Hall-M9181.jpg|thumb|275 px|Engaku-ji, a building with old-style ''katōmado'']] {{nihongo|'''''Katōmado'''''|火灯窓|lit. fire light window}}, also written {{nihongo|as|花頭窓・華頭窓|lit. flower top window}}, is a style of pointed arch or bell-shaped window found in [[:en:Japanese architecture|Japanese architecture]].<ref group="note">In English, this type of window is also simply called " window".</ref> It first arrived in Japan from [[:en:China|China]] together with [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism,<ref name="architecture">{{cite book |author=Coaldrake, William Howard |title=Architecture and authority in Japan |publisher=Routledge |location=New York |year=1996 |isbn=0-415-10601-X }}</ref> as an element of [[:en:Zenshūyō|Zen style]] architecture, but from the end of the 16th century it started to be used in temples of other [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] sects, [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]s, [[:en:Japanese castle|castles]], and [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] residences as well.<ref name="jaanus">{{cite web|last=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|title=Katoumado|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/k/katoumado.htm|publisher=JAANUS|access-date=2010-06-21}}</ref> The window initially was not flared, but its design and shape changed over time: the two vertical frames were widened and curves were added at the bottom.<ref name="jaanus"/> The ''kanji'' characters used for its name have also changed through the centuries, from the original "fire window" to "flower head window".<ref name="urlKyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window">{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/19.html |title=Kyoto City Web / Preview Kyoto / Kato-mado Window |access-date=1 July 2010}}</ref>
The oldest extant example of ''katōmado'' can be found in [[Engaku-ji]]'s ''Shariden'' (Relic Hall)<ref group="note">This hall was moved to its current location in 1563.(source: {{cite web |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110006941481 |title=Engakuji Shariden wo Rei to Shita Rekishiteki Kikōzōbutsu|access-date=1 July 2010}}) The original is believed to have been built sometime between the late 14th century and the mid 15th century.(source: {{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/603 |title=National Treasure Database of Japan (国指定文化財等データベース:各棟情報詳細)|access-date=1 July 2010}})</ref> in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], which is thought to closely follow the original style as it was introduced to Japan, with the vertical frames touching the bottom in straight lines.<ref name="jaanus"/> Another well-known example can be found in the room called {{nihongo|Genji-no-ma|源氏の間}} in the [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] at [[Ishiyama-dera]], [[:en:Shiga prefecture|Shiga prefecture]]. For this reason, ''katōmado'' are also known as {{nihongo|''genjimado''|源氏窓|[[:en:Genji (era)|Genji]] window}}.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
